content.problemStatement:
You are given NNN points in DDD-dimensional space. The ithi^{th}ith point has DDD coordinates - xi,1,xi,2,…,xi,Dx_{i, 1}, x_{i, 2}, \ldots, x_{i, D}xi,1​,xi,2​,…,xi,D​.

Consider a weighted undirected complete graph with these NNN points as its vertices. Weight of the edge between points iii and jjj is ∣xi,1−xj,1∣+∣xi,2−xj,2∣+…+∣xi,D−xj,D∣|x_{i,1} - x_{j, 1}| + |x_{i,2} - x_{j, 2}| + \ldots + |x_{i, D} - x_{j,D}|∣xi,1​−xj,1​∣+∣xi,2​−xj,2​∣+…+∣xi,D​−xj,D​∣.

Find the weight of the maximum spanning tree of this graph.

###Input:


The first line contains two integers N,DN, DN,D.
The iii-th of the next NNN lines contains a description of the iii-th point. It has DDD integers: xi,1,xi,2,…,xi,Dx_{i, 1}, x_{i, 2}, \ldots, x_{i,D}xi,1​,xi,2​,…,xi,D​.
###Output:
Output a single line which contains a single integer - the Maximum Spanning Tree Weight

###Constraints


1≤D≤51 \leq D \leq 51≤D≤5
1≤N≤200 0001 \leq N \leq 200\,0001≤N≤200000
0≤xi,j≤100 0000 \leq x_{i, j} \leq 100\,0000≤xi,j​≤100000
###Subtasks



10 points, N≤5000N \leq 5000N≤5000




90 points, N≤200 000N \leq 200\,000N≤200000
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T – the number of test cases. Each test case starts with a line containing two integers N and D (1 ≤ N ≤ 200000, 1 ≤ D ≤ 5). Then follow N lines, each containing D integers xi,1 … xi,D (0 ≤ xi,j ≤ 1000000) describing the coordinates of the i‑th point.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing one integer – the total weight of a maximum spanning tree of the complete graph whose edge weights are Manhattan distances between the points.

solution.approach:
For a Manhattan distance in D dimensions (D ≤ 5) the farthest point of any vertex can be found among the 2^D extreme points obtained by taking the maximum of the linear forms Σ (±xk). For each mask m (0 … 2^D‑1) we compute the vertex with the largest value of Σ (sign_m(k) * xk). Let maxIdx[m] be that vertex. For every vertex v we create an edge (v, maxIdx[m]) for all masks. The weight of the edge is the Manhattan distance between the two vertices. It can be shown that for any cut the maximum‑weight crossing edge is always among these edges, therefore a maximum spanning tree of the complete graph is also a maximum spanning tree of this reduced graph. We collect all such edges (at most N·2^D ≤ 6.4·10^6), sort them in decreasing order of weight and run Kruskal’s algorithm with a DSU to pick N‑1 edges. The sum of the chosen edge weights is the answer.

Steps per test case:
1. Read all points.
2. For each mask (0 … 2^D‑1) compute the projection Σ (±xk) for every point and keep the index with the maximum projection.
3. Build the edge list: for each point i and each mask m add edge (i, maxIdx[m]) with weight = Manhattan distance(i, maxIdx[m]).
4. Sort edges descending.
5. Initialise DSU of size N, total = 0, taken = 0.
6. Iterate edges; if the ends belong to different components, unite them, add weight to total, increment taken. Stop when taken == N‑1.
7. Output total.

The algorithm runs in O(N·2^D + E·logE) time where E = N·2^D, and uses O(N·2^D) memory for the edge list, both easily fitting the limits.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N·2^D) for storing projections and edges (≈ 6.4·10^6 elements, ~100 MB).
